questions.question_text: Massachusetts fair housing law protects which class that is NOT protected under federal fair housing law?
distractors: Race
Familial status
National origin
questions.explanation: Source of income (including housing vouchers/Section 8) is protected under MA Chapter 151B but not under the federal Fair Housing Act. Race, familial status, and national origin are federal protections.

questions.question_text: A landlord refuses to rent to a tenant who plans to pay with a Section 8 housing voucher. Under Massachusetts law, this is:
questions.correct_answer: Illegal discrimination based on source of income
distractors: Legal since vouchers are optional
Legal if the landlord has financial concerns
Legal for buildings with fewer than 4 units
questions.explanation: Massachusetts protects source of income as a class under Chapter 151B. Refusing to rent based on a tenant's use of Section 8 vouchers is housing discrimination regardless of property size.

questions.question_text: Under Chapter 151B, discrimination based on marital status is prohibited. Which scenario violates this protection?
questions.correct_answer: Refusing to rent to an unmarried couple
distractors: Offering family-sized units to married couples
Marketing a unit as ideal for families
Providing information about nearby schools
questions.explanation: Marital status is a protected class in Massachusetts. Refusing to rent to someone based on their marital status, including unmarried couples, is illegal discrimination.

questions.question_text: Which Massachusetts-specific protected class covers persons aged 40 and older?
distractors: Senior status
Elderly classification
Retirement status
questions.explanation: Massachusetts Chapter 151B protects age (persons 40 and over) as a distinct protected class. This goes beyond federal law, which does not include age as a protected class in housing.

questions.question_text: A landlord refuses to rent to a tenant because of their military veteran status. Under Massachusetts law, this is:
distractors: Legal since veteran status is not protected
Prohibited only in federally funded housing
Legal for landlords with fewer than 4 units
questions.explanation: Military/veteran status is a protected class under Massachusetts Chapter 151B. This protection goes beyond federal fair housing law, which does not include veteran status.

questions.question_text: The federal 'Mrs. Murphy' exemption covers owner-occupied buildings with up to four units. Massachusetts narrows this to:
distractors: Owner-occupied three-family dwellings
Owner-occupied single-family homes only
No owner-occupied exemption exists in MA
questions.explanation: Massachusetts has a narrower exemption than federal law, applying only to owner-occupied two-family dwellings. The federal exemption covers up to four units.

questions.question_text: A religious organization gives housing preference to members of its own faith. Under Massachusetts law, this is:
distractors: Always prohibited in Massachusetts
Permitted only with Board approval
Legal for all types of housing
questions.explanation: Religious organizations may give preference to members of the same religion for non-commercial housing. This is a specific exemption under Massachusetts fair housing law.

questions.question_text: An owner of a two-family home who lives in one unit advertises 'No families with children.' The owner:
questions.correct_answer: Has lost the exemption by using discriminatory advertising
distractors: Is protected by the owner-occupied exemption
Is exempt since it is a two-family property
Can advertise preferences as the owner-occupant
questions.explanation: Even when the owner-occupied two-family exemption applies, it does NOT apply if discriminatory advertising is used. Using discriminatory ads forfeits the exemption.

questions.question_text: Compared to federal fair housing law, Massachusetts exemptions are generally:
distractors: Broader and more permissive
Identical to federal exemptions
Applied on a case-by-case basis only
questions.explanation: Massachusetts has narrower exemptions than federal law. The state is more protective of tenants and buyers, with fewer situations where discrimination is legally excused.

questions.question_text: A church owns a 10-unit apartment building operated as a for-profit rental business. Can it give preference to church members?
questions.correct_answer: No, the exemption applies only to non-commercial housing
distractors: Yes, religious organizations always have this right
Yes, for up to 4 units in the building
Yes, if all tenants agree to the preference
questions.explanation: The religious organization exemption applies only to non-commercial housing. A for-profit rental business does not qualify, even if owned by a religious organization.

questions.question_text: A person who believes they experienced housing discrimination in Massachusetts should file a complaint with:
distractors: The Board of Registration of Real Estate
The local police department or sheriff
The state Attorney General's office only
questions.explanation: MCAD (Massachusetts Commission Against Discrimination) investigates complaints of housing discrimination. Complaints can also be filed with HUD federally, but MCAD is the state agency.

questions.question_text: A tenant experiences discrimination on January 15, 2026. What is the latest date they can file a complaint with MCAD?
distractors: January 15, 2027
March 15, 2026
questions.explanation: Complaints must generally be filed within 300 days of the discriminatory act. January 15 plus 300 days = November 11, 2026.

questions.question_text: MCAD can award which types of relief in a housing discrimination case?
questions.correct_answer: Damages, injunctive relief, and civil penalties
distractors: Damages only, no injunctive relief
Criminal penalties and jail time
Injunctive relief only, no monetary damages
questions.explanation: MCAD can award damages (compensatory and emotional distress), injunctive relief (orders to stop discriminatory conduct), and civil penalties. MCAD does not impose criminal penalties.

questions.question_text: MCAD receives a pattern of complaints against a property management company. Can MCAD investigate without an individual complaint?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, MCAD can initiate its own investigations
distractors: No, a specific complaint is always required
Only if the AG refers the matter
Only with a court order
questions.explanation: MCAD can initiate investigations on its own, even without an individual complaint. This self-initiation power helps address systemic or pattern discrimination.

questions.question_text: After receiving a housing discrimination complaint, MCAD has the authority to:
questions.correct_answer: Hold hearings and issue binding orders
distractors: Only mediate between the parties
Only refer the case to civil court
Only issue advisory opinions
questions.explanation: MCAD can hold hearings and issue orders that are legally binding. This quasi-judicial authority makes MCAD a powerful enforcement body for fair housing violations.
